era_context: Akkadian Empire in collapse; Gutian invasions dominating Mesopotamia; Egyptian Old Kingdom has ended, First Intermediate Period beginning; Indus Valley Civilization at peak development; Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Anatolia.

events.title: Egyptian First Intermediate Period begins; Old Kingdom definitively ended
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty has ended (traditional date c. 2181 BCE, though some scholars place it c. 2150 BCE). Egypt enters the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE), characterized by the fragmentation of central authority and the rise of competing regional dynasties. The Seventh and Eighth Dynasties (Memphis-based, weak) and the Ninth and Tenth Dynasties (Heracleopolitan, stronger) emerge as rival powers. Upper Egypt and Lower Egypt are effectively independent. The unified state that had endured for over 500 years has collapsed.
key_figures: Pepi II (last Sixth Dynasty pharaoh, traditional dates 2278–2184 BCE)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on First Intermediate Period

events.certainty_note: The end of the Old Kingdom is traditionally dated c. 2181 BCE, but some scholars place it c. 2150 BCE. Dating is uncertain by ±20–30 years.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: Gutian invasions complete; Mesopotamian political fragmentation
events.description: The Akkadian Empire has effectively collapsed. Gutian forces from the Zagros Mountains have invaded and occupied much of Mesopotamia. The Akkadian state, which had dominated the region for over a century under Sargon and his successors, is reduced to scattered city-states. Southern Mesopotamian cities (Lagash, Uruk, Ur) reassert independence or fall under Gutian control. The period of Gutian dominance (c. 2154–2112 BCE, traditional dates) begins. This represents one of the earliest documented cases of imperial collapse.
key_figures: Shar-kali-sharri (last Akkadian king, traditional dates 2217–2193 BCE)

sources.name: Royal inscriptions and king lists (Sumerian King List)
Cuneiform administrative records showing collapse of central authority
Archaeological evidence of destruction layers in Mesopotamian sites

events.certainty_note: The Akkadian collapse is well-documented in cuneiform sources and archaeology. Dating is uncertain by ±10–20 years.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: peak urban development; trade networks extensive
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues at its peak. Major urban centres (Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, Kalibangan) are densely populated with sophisticated urban planning, standardized weights and measures, and extensive trade networks. Maritime trade with Mesopotamia continues, though the Akkadian collapse may be beginning to disrupt these networks. The civilization's internal organization remains unknown due to the undeciphered script.
events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's existence and flourishing is archaeologically confirmed; absolute dating is ±50–100 years.

events.title: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Anatolia: urban development and trade
events.description: The Early Bronze Age III (c. 2600–2300 BCE in some chronologies, c. 2300–2000 BCE in others) is underway in the Levant and Anatolia. Urban centres like Ebla in Syria are flourishing as trade hubs. The collapse of the Akkadian Empire may be beginning to disrupt trade networks, but local city-states continue to develop. Anatolia is experiencing increasing urbanization and trade integration.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Ebla, Tell Mardikh, and other Levantine sites

events.certainty_note: The Early Bronze Age III is archaeologically confirmed; absolute dating is uncertain by ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Old Kingdom's end and the First Intermediate Period's beginning is debated. Some scholars place the transition c. 2181 BCE, others c. 2150 BCE, with uncertainty of ±20–30 years. The 'Akkadian drought' hypothesis (Weiss et al., 1993) linking the Akkadian collapse to climate change is contested; some scholars emphasize political and military factors. The precise dating of the Gutian invasion and dominance period remains uncertain. The Early Bronze Age III chronology in the Levant and Anatolia is debated, with different scholars proposing different date ranges.
historiographic_note: This year marks a major transition point in world history: the simultaneous collapse of two major empires (Akkad and Egypt's Old Kingdom) and the beginning of new political orders. However, the historical record is fragmentary and dating is uncertain. The collapse of the Akkadian Empire is one of the earliest documented cases of imperial collapse, and the causes (climate change, military invasion, internal rebellion) remain debated. The Egyptian First Intermediate Period is known primarily from tomb inscriptions and later king lists, with limited contemporary documentation. The Indus Valley Civilization, by contrast, appears unaffected by these upheavals, suggesting regional isolation or resilience. The Levant and Anatolia are poorly documented in written sources for this period, with knowledge derived primarily from archaeology.

graph_edges.note: First Intermediate Period eventually leads to Middle Kingdom reunification
Gutian period eventually leads to Ur III renaissance
Peak Indus development eventually leads to civilization's decline
